turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 16-1220, Animal Science Products v. Hebei Welcome Pharmaceuticals.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: When foreign law is relevant to a case in the US Federal Court, the Federal Court is sometimes presented with the foreign government's own views about the meaning of its law. The question presented in this case, when a foreign government submits a statement saying what its law conveys, is the Federal Court ordinarily required to treat the submission as conclusive? We hold that it is not. The Federal Court should accord respectful consideration to a foreign government's submission, but the Court is not bound to accord conclusive effect to the foreign government's statements. Plaintiff, petitioners in this case before us are US purchasers of vitamin C. They sued defendant, respondents Chinese sellers of the nutrient. The US purchasers allege that the Chinese sellers had agreed to fix the price and quantity of vitamin C exported to the United States in violation of US Antitrust Law. The Chinese sellers moved to dismiss the complaint on the ground that Chinese law compelled them to fix the price and quantity of vitamin C exports. This they asserted shielded them from liability under the US Antitrust Law. Chinese Ministry of Commerce submitted to the US District Court an amicus brief conveying that Chinese law required exporters of vitamin C to engage in price-fixing. The US purchasers countered with evidence supporting a contrary reading of Chinese law, including Chinese submission to the World Trade Organization stating it had given up export administration of vitamin C. The District Court denied the Chinese sellers' motion to dismiss the complaint, but the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit reversed. When a foreign government submits a statement on the meaning and interpretation of its domestic law, the Court of Appeals held the Federal Court is bound to defer to that construction whenever the construction is reasonable. We granted review. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 44.1 specifies that a determination of foreign law must be treated as a ruling on the question of law rather than as a finding of fact as had been the common law practice. Correspondingly, in determining federal law, the Court may consider any relevant material or source, whether or not submitted by a party. Neither Rule 44.1 nor any other rule or statute addresses the weight a federal court determining foreign law should give to the views presented by the foreign government whose law is in contention. In the spirit of international comity, the Court should carefully consider a foreign state's views about the meaning of its law, but the appropriate weight in each case will depend on the circumstances. Given the world's diverse legal systems and the range of circumstances in which a foreign government's views may be presented, no single formula will fit all cases. Relevant considerations include the statement's clarity, thoroughness, and support; its context and purpose; the transparency of the foreign legal system; the role and authority of the entity or official offering the statement; and the statement's consistency with the foreign government's past positions. Judges in this light, the Court of Appeals erred in holding that the Chinese Ministry's submission is binding so long as it is facially reasonable. That unyielding rule is inconsistent with all things considered policy of Rule 44.1. As the opinion explains, the Court of Appeals rule is likewise inconsistent with this Court's treatment of analogous submissions from States of the United States, nor as the Court of Appeals suppose is the binding if facially reasonable rule required by decisions of this Court or by international comity. Because the Court of Appeals concluded that the District Court was bound to defer to the Chinese Ministry's brief, the Court did not consider the materials identified by the District Court, which were at least relevant to the weight the Ministry's submission should receive. We therefore vacate the judgment of the Court of Appeals and remand the case for renewed consideration. The Court's opinion is unanimous.
